rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF STEPHEN J. OUTLAW
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion To Compel Responses To Specially Prepared Interrogatories (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [2 of 2] Hernandez and Escalante's request for sanctions is DENIED. They did not show that Outlaw's opposition to their motion was "unsuccessful[l]." See CCP Section 2030.300(d). Moreover, the issue of sanctions is not properly presented. CCP Section 2023.040 provides: "A request for a sanction shall, in the notice of motion, identify every person, party, and attorney against whom the sanction is sought, and specify the type of sanction sought." Hernandez and Escalante failed to include a request for sanctions in their notice of motion. Nor did their notice identify every person, party, and attorney against whom the sanction is sought. They mention sanctions in their memorandum. But even there, they did not identify every person, party, and attorney against whom the sanction is sought. Outlaw shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. Petitioner is ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative by the time set for this hearing. Remote appearance is available in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF STEPHEN J. OUTLAW
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Responses To Specially Prepared Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: [1 of 2] This tentative ruling is issued by Hon. Joseph M. Quinn. Petitioner Cruz Outlaw is the personal representative of the estate of her deceased husband, Stephen J. Outlaw. Jose A. Hernandez is Cruz Outlaw's son and Enna S. Escalante is Hernandez's wife. Cruz Outlaw filed a Petition for Return of Property to the Estate Pursuant to California Probate Code Section 850 and for an Order Finding Financial and Physical Elder Abuse Pursuant to Welfare and institution's Code Section 15610.70 et. Seq [sic]. Hernandez and Escalante are respondents on that petition. The court denied the elder abuse claims without prejudice; the remainder of the petition is pending. In the remaining claim, Outlaw alleges that Hernandez and Escalante took over a coin collection that belonged to the estate, sold it, and used the proceeds to by their home; they must now reimburse the estate or surrender equity in their home to the estate. Hernandez and Escalante deny the allegations. Trial is set for May 10, 2022. Now before the court is Hernandez and Escalante's Motion to Compel Answers to the Request for Specially Prepared Interrogatories, Set One. The motion is DENIED. If responding party fails to respond, all that need be shown in the moving papers is that a set of interrogatories was properly served on the opposing party, that the time to respond has expired, and that no response of any kind has been served. See Leach v. Sup. Ct. (Markum) (1980) 111 Cal.App.3d 902, 905-906. No separate statement is required and the moving party is not required to show a reasonable and good faith attempt to resolve the matter informally with opposing counsel. CCP Section 2030.290; Sinaiko Healthcare Consulting, Inc. v. Pacific Healthcare Consultants, (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 411. The basic elements of interrogatories are set out in part in CCP Section 2030.060(b): "In the first paragraph immediately below the title of the case, there shall appear the identity of the propounding party, the set number, and the identity of the responding party." Here, Hernandez and Escalante's interrogatories did not correctly state the title of the case, did not identify the propounding party or the responding party, and did not identify the court or the case number. One would have had to read through to page four to discover that the interrogatories may actually relate to this case. Hernandez and Escalante's counsel realized his mistake prior to filing this motion; rather than serve proper interrogatories, he decided to move to compel based on the clearly deficient Set One. It is true that Outlaw's counsel requested a copy of the interrogatories and soon received a copy via email. A professional response may have been to bring the errors to the attention of opposing counsel. However, on this motion, Hernandez and Escalante bear the burden of persuasion and they have not demonstrated that the interrogatories they served-Set One-triggered a duty to respond. On this record, the court finds that the deficiencies are not excusable.
